title: run_pipeline completion saves stale provision-time last_accessed_at, rewinding the TTL clock by the run duration
claim: run_pipeline saves the TenantSchema at completion with update_fields=['state','last_accessed_at'] using the in-memory instance whose last_accessed_at was captured at provision time (run start), clobbering any newer touch made during the load and starting the 24h TTL clock H hours in the past for an H-hour pipeline — a smaller re-opening of the 2026-06-10 'janitor drops fresh schema' incident class for long loads plus quiet users.

votes.evidence: Re-traced independently. (1) mcp_server/services/materializer.py:183 `tenant_schema = SchemaManager().provision(tenant_membership.tenant)` — this in-memory object's last_accessed_at is fixed at provision/run-start time: in schema_manager.py the fresh/resurrect path sets `ts.last_accessed_at = timezone.now()` (line 121) and the existing-ACTIVE path returns `existing` after `existing.touch()` (line 77), which also sets it on the returned instance. (2) No re-fetch of `tenant_schema` occurs between line 183 and line 486 across the whole LOAD+TRANSFORM (grep of `tenant_schema` in the file shows only the provision assignment, the run creation, _load_prior_resume_cursors, and the line-486 save). (3) materializer.py:485-486: `tenant_schema.state = "active"; tenant_schema.save(update_fields=["state", "last_accessed_at"])` persists the stale provision-time timestamp. apps/workspaces/models.py:40 `last_accessed_at = models.DateTimeField(null=True, blank=True)` — no auto_now, so the literal in-memory value is written, NOT refreshed to now. (4) Consequence path is real: apps/workspaces/tasks.py:535 `cutoff = timezone.now() - timedelta(hours=settings.SCHEMA_TTL_HOURS)` with config/settings/base.py:345 `SCHEMA_TTL_HOURS = 24`, then expire_inactive_schemas filters `state=ACTIVE, last_accessed_at__lt=cutoff` (tasks.py:538-540) on a */30 cron. So the schema's effective expiry deadline becomes provision_time+24h rather than completion_time+24h, losing the run duration H. Mechanism CONFIRMED for the materialize_tenants path.
votes.notes:
PARTIAL because the finding overstates blast radius: run_pipeline has two live callers (grep of `run_pipeline` in apps/mcp_server). Path A = refresh_tenant_schema (tasks.py:173) is SELF-HEALING — immediately after run_pipeline returns, Step 3 at tasks.py:182-184 sets `new_schema.last_accessed_at = timezone.now(); await new_schema.asave(update_fields=["state","last_accessed_at"])` on the same row, overwriting the stale provision-time value. The finding does not note this; the defect only manifests via Path B = materialize_tenants -> _run_pipeline_with_progress (tasks.py:277-282, 496) where there is NO post-pipeline re-touch (verified tasks.py:294-353: after the per-tenant loop only view-schema rebuilds run, no per-tenant last_accessed_at refresh). So 1 of 2 callers is affected, not both.

The "clobbers any newer touch made during the load" sub-claim is technically reachable but secondary: during the load the schema row is ACTIVE in the DB (provision() sets ACTIVE at schema_manager.py:120 with no MATERIALIZING transition), and workspace_service.py:95 atouch() / :103 aupdate touch ACTIVE/MATERIALIZING schemas, so a concurrent user action could DB-write a newer last_accessed_at that line 486's update_fields save then clobbers. This is a narrow race, not the dominant effect; the dominant effect is the deterministic H-hour rewind.

Severity DEBT (not BROKEN-NOW) is correct: under normal short pipelines H is minutes so the lost window is negligible; only very long Connect/OCS exports (hours) materially shorten the 24h TTL, and a schema is only actually dropped if the user is also quiet for the remaining window. No mid-flight drop occurs because last_accessed_at is fresh at provision time. impact=correctness is appropriate (it can cause premature teardown / data unavailability requiring re-materialization, but no silent data corruption). The 2026-06-10 incident analogy is apt but this is a strictly milder variant. Fix is trivial: at materializer.py:485-486 set `tenant_schema.last_accessed_at = timezone.now()` before saving (mirroring the existing tasks.py:183 and schema_manager.py:121 patterns), or re-fetch/touch() the row.

title: User-merge conflict path cascade-deletes the duplicate's TenantMetadata and can orphan connection wiring
claim: _merge_tenant_memberships deletes the duplicate user's conflicting TenantMembership rows when the canonical already has that tenant; TenantMetadata is a OneToOne on TenantMembership with on_delete=CASCADE, so discovered provider metadata (including team info) riding on the duplicate's membership is destroyed even when the canonical membership carries none — defeating the model's stated purpose of surviving teardown to skip re-discovery. The surviving membership can also be left connection=None when only the duplicate's was wired to a credential. Recoverable via re-discovery on next materialization; rare, transactional.

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced the full chain and reproduced the core defect, but one attribution in the claim is imprecise.

CONFIRMED mechanism:
- `apps/workspaces/models.py:266-270`: `TenantMetadata.tenant_membership = models.OneToOneField("users.TenantMembership", on_delete=models.CASCADE, related_name="metadata")`. Docstring (models.py:262-264): "Survives schema teardown so re-provisioning can skip re-discovery."
- `apps/users/services/merge.py:165-166`: `dup_qs = TenantMembership.objects.filter(user=duplicate)` then `conflict_deleted, _ = dup_qs.filter(tenant_id__in=canonical_tenant_ids).delete()`. The `.delete()` on the membership CASCADE-deletes its OneToOne `TenantMetadata`. So when canonical already has a membership for that tenant but no metadata, and the duplicate's membership carried metadata, the metadata is destroyed. Trigger requires both users to have a membership for the SAME tenant (`TenantMembership.unique_together=[["user","tenant"]]`, models.py:226).
- Recoverable claim CONFIRMED: `mcp_server/services/materializer.py:539-542` `_run_discover_phase` does `TenantMetadata.objects.update_or_create(tenant_membership=tenant_membership, defaults={"metadata":..., "discovered_at":...})` on every materialization where `pipeline.has_metadata_discovery`. The next materialization re-creates metadata on the canonical's surviving membership.
- Materiality CONFIRMED: lost metadata is read at `apps/agents/graph/base.py:263-265` (scoped `tenant_membership__tenant=tenant, tenant_membership__user=user` — canonical with no metadata returns None) and `apps/workspaces/api/views.py:196` / `mcp_server/server.py:210,270` (tenant-scoped — survives if any other user has metadata for that tenant, else None).

CONFIRMED connection-orphan mechanism (with nuance):
- `_merge_tenant_memberships` runs BEFORE `_merge_tenant_connections` (merge.py:337-342). The duplicate's conflicting membership is deleted first, so it is no longer in `conn.memberships` when `_merge_tenant_connections` does `conn.memberships.update(connection=existing)` (merge.py:191). The canonical's surviving membership keeps its own `connection` and is never re-wired to the duplicate's working credential. If canonical's membership had `connection=None`, it stays None → `aresolve_credential` returns None (`apps/users/services/credential_resolver.py:75-77`), so materialization can't authenticate. Recoverable on next OAuth login via `resolve_*` which does `aget_or_create(user, tenant)` then `tm.connection = conn` (`apps/users/services/tenant_resolution.py:57-60, 104-107, ~162`).

Reachability CONFIRMED LIVE: `merge_users` is wired to `pre_social_login` (`apps/users/apps.py:24`, called at `apps/users/signals.py:119`) firing on every OAuth login, plus the `merge_duplicate_users` operator command. Whole merge runs in `transaction.atomic()` (merge.py:331).

WHY PARTIAL (correction): the claim says the CASCADE destroys "discovered provider metadata (including team info)." That conflates two distinct losses. Team info (`team_slug`/`team_name`) lives in `TenantMembership.provider_metadata` JSON ON THE MEMBERSHIP ROW ITSELF (`apps/users/models.py:216-251`), not in the CASCADE-deleted `TenantMetadata`. The team JSON is lost because the membership row is directly deleted (merge.py:166), not via the OneToOne CASCADE. The CASCADE specifically destroys the separate `TenantMetadata` discovery blob (form/case definitions, Connect visit counts). Both are lost, but via two different mechanisms — the finding attributes both to the OneToOne CASCADE.
votes.notes: Defect is real, reachable on a live path, transactional, and rare (requires both canonical+duplicate to hold a membership for the same tenant, with metadata/credential asymmetry favoring the duplicate). LATENT/correctness severity is accurate. PARTIAL rather than CONFIRMED only because the claim mis-attributes the team-info loss to the TenantMetadata OneToOne CASCADE — team info lives in TenantMembership.provider_metadata and dies with the membership row itself, while the CASCADE destroys the separate TenantMetadata discovery blob. Both losses are real; the mechanism description is imprecise. Fix would be to repoint/preserve the duplicate's TenantMetadata and connection onto the surviving canonical membership before deleting (or skip-delete when canonical's membership lacks metadata/connection and the duplicate's has it).

title: Connect resumable writers can duplicate on stale-cursor replay; safety rests on an implicit invariant that the zombie-janitor fix would break
claim: Five resumable Connect tables (completed_works, payments, invoices, assessments, completed_modules) have surrogate IDENTITY PKs and no ON CONFLICT, so any page replay re-inserts silently. Replay is currently prevented only because hard-crashed runs stay LOADING (never PARTIAL/FAILED), so _load_prior_resume_cursors forces a clean DROP+reload; cursor_state is persisted per page after commit and lags committed rows. Adding the recommended zombie-run janitor without nulling cursor_state, or any future out-of-process FAILED flip, creates a real duplication path. Becomes reachable if the zombie-janitor fix lands naively.

votes.evidence:
The finding's central premise — that the five tables are "resumable Connect tables" whose safety rests on an implicit LOADING-state invariant — is false. They are explicitly and statically marked non-resumable, and the duplication path the finding describes is unreachable today and would remain unreachable even after a naive zombie-janitor FAILED flip.

(1) connect_sync.yml:17-31 sets `resumable: false` on completed_works, payments, invoices, assessments, completed_modules (and users). pipeline_registry.py:_parse_pipeline reads `s.get("resumable", True)`, so these five SourceConfig.resumable are False.

(2) materializer.py:264 `source_is_resumable = is_resumable_provider and source.resumable` → False for all five. Line 265 `start_cursor = prior_cursors.get(source.name) if source_is_resumable else None` → start_cursor is ALWAYS None for these tables, regardless of any prior run's state. Line 283-287 sets `cursor_callback = None` for them.

(3) In the writer (materializer.py:1593-1594, _write_connect_completed_works, identical pattern in the other four), `if start_cursor is None: DROP TABLE ... CASCADE`. Since start_cursor is always None, every run is a full DROP+CREATE+INSERT. No accumulation across runs, so no duplication. Line 1657 `if cursor_callback is not None and max_id is not None: cursor_callback(...)` never fires (callback is None), so cursor_state is never persisted for these tables.

(4) The CREATE TABLE uses `id BIGINT GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY` (materializer.py:1599 etc.) and the INSERT has no ON CONFLICT — both true as the finding states — but these are surrogate keys precisely because the v2 export emits no per-row `id`. _max_id (materializer.py:~1248) returns None when no row has an integer id, so even on the per-page-commit code path no watermark could ever be recorded.

(5) prior_cursors (materializer.py:252) is consumed ONLY at line 265 (verified via grep — exactly two references), and _load_prior_resume_cursors (564-605) only returns a cursor when `cursor_state.last_id` is an int — never set for these tables. So a zombie-janitor flipping a run to FAILED cannot create the path: the cursor map would not contain these sources, and line 265 would discard it anyway because source_is_resumable=False.

Git history makes the design explicit and intentional. Commit f26c1a0 (2026-05-28) attempted the exact fix the finding "recommends" (id BIGINT PRIMARY KEY + ON CONFLICT (id) DO NOTHING on all five tables). Commit 2587158 (also 2026-05-28, later) then discovered the v2 export emits NO per-row id — so that PK was always-null and every insert crashed — and reverted to surrogate IDENTITY PKs, dropped the meaningless ON CONFLICT, and marked all five sources `resumable: false`. Current regression tests test_payments_idless_full_reload_no_duplication and test_completed_works_idless_full_reload_no_duplication (tests/test_materializer.py:1199,1281) assert "non-resumable writers must DROP/CREATE each run," locking in the current safe design.
votes.notes:
The finding misidentifies the safety mechanism. It is NOT the implicit "hard-crashed runs stay LOADING" invariant. It is the EXPLICIT static `resumable: false` YAML flag, which hard-gates source_is_resumable (materializer.py:264) and forces start_cursor=None → DROP+CREATE every run (1593) and cursor_callback=None (no watermark). This gate is independent of run state, so the hypothesized "zombie-janitor flips run to FAILED → duplication" chain cannot occur: line 265 discards any prior cursor for non-resumable sources unconditionally.

The ON CONFLICT absence the finding flags is real but harmless because the table is dropped every run and the per-page-commit path is never exercised for these sources (start_cursor always None, callback always None, export has no id). It is dead-but-correct code with a latent footgun ONLY in the purely hypothetical scenario where someone flips these sources to `resumable: true` in the YAML — which would also require the upstream export to start emitting a stable integer id (it does not). That double precondition makes this COSMETIC/documentation-level, not a real LATENT correctness bug.

If anything is worth a small hardening note: a comment near _RESUMABLE_CONNECT_SOURCES (materializer.py:772) explaining that membership there only routes the writer through the per-page path and that the YAML resumable flag is the real gate, since the two lists disagree (the frozenset includes all five "non-resumable" tables) and that disagreement is what makes the code look dangerous at a glance. But there is no actionable defect on a live route, agent tool, or scheduled task today.

title: Transform assets execute arbitrary user SQL via dbt as the managed-DB superuser — no validation, no SET ROLE downgrade, no schema confinement
claim: TransformationAsset.sql_content is free-text (only name is regex-validated) and writable via the serializer; the executor runs it through dbt connecting as the full MANAGED_DATABASE_URL role (the superuser/owner that runs CREATE SCHEMA/CREATE ROLE) with no SET ROLE downgrade, no search_path lockdown, and no SQL validation — unlike the query MCP tool (SET ROLE _ro + validator). A tenant member can POST a scope=TENANT asset whose body reads another tenant's schema (SELECT * FROM t_victim.raw_cases) or does DDL/COPY/create roles via jinja run_query, which dbt materializes into the attacker's own schema for normal readonly retrieval; it runs automatically on the next materialization's tenant stage or via POST /api/transformations/runs/trigger/. Complete bypass of the read-only/tenant-isolation guarantees the query path enforces.
chain: POST /api/transformations/assets/ (views.py:54 IsAuthenticated, own-tenant; models.py:100 validates only name) -> next materialization tenant stage (executor.py:50-73) -> generate_profiles_yml uses MANAGED_DATABASE_URL superuser, no search_path/SET ROLE (executor.py:134, dbt_runner.py:28) -> dbt executes fully-qualified cross-tenant SQL -> materializes victim data into attacker schema -> read via normal readonly path

votes.evidence:
I re-traced the full chain from the actual files and reproduced it end to end.

WRITABLE FREE-TEXT SQL: apps/transformations/serializers.py:9-23 lists "sql_content" in `fields` and only `["id","created_by","created_at","updated_at"]` in `read_only_fields` — so sql_content is client-writable. apps/transformations/models.py:23-32 puts a RegexValidator ONLY on `name`; sql_content (49-51) is a bare TextField. models.py clean() (104-114) validates only scope/tenant/workspace consistency, never the SQL body. No validate_sql_content anywhere.

REACHABLE, LOW-PRIVILEGE ENTRY: config/urls.py:96 mounts apps.transformations.urls; urls.py:7-8 registers `assets` and `runs` routers => POST /api/transformations/assets/ and POST /api/transformations/runs/trigger/ are live. views.py:30 permission is IsAuthenticated. perform_create (54-59) blocks only scope==SYSTEM; _check_write_permission (74-87) requires only tenant MEMBERSHIP for a TENANT asset (workspace path needs read_write/manage, but the tenant path has no role gate). trigger() (121-166) likewise requires only `request.user.tenant_memberships.filter(tenant=tenant).exists()` (line 137). So any authenticated tenant member can both create and synchronously execute a TENANT-scoped asset.

VERBATIM SQL INTO DBT: apps/transformations/services/dbt_project.py:49-50 writes `asset.sql_content` directly to models/{name}.sql with no sanitization. dbt renders jinja in model files, so run_query/statement blocks are available => arbitrary SQL/DDL at run time.

PRIVILEGED CONNECTION, NO DOWNGRADE: executor.py:134-141 reads MANAGED_DATABASE_URL and calls generate_profiles_yml. mcp_server/services/dbt_runner.py:42-60 builds the postgres profile using that URL's `user`/`password` directly — no SET ROLE, no role swap. This is the SAME credential apps/workspaces/services/schema_manager.py uses to run `CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS` (100,141,160), `CREATE ROLE {} NOLOGIN` (605), `ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES` and `GRANT` (609-623) — i.e. the privileged managed/owner role. Contrast the query path: mcp_server/services/query.py:44 does `SET ROLE {readonly_role}` (schema_name+"_ro", context.py:29-31) and execute_query() runs the SQLValidator first. The transform path has neither guard.

AUTO-EXECUTION: mcp_server/services/materializer.py:448-454 — the TRANSFORM stage of every materialization checks `TransformationAsset.objects.filter(tenant=...).exists()` and calls _run_transform_phase -> run_transformation_pipeline (1060-1065). executor.py:50-79 runs the SYSTEM and TENANT stages filtered by that tenant, so an attacker's TENANT asset for their own tenant executes automatically on the next data load, in addition to the manual trigger endpoint.

EXFIL READBACK CONFIRMED: schema_manager._create_readonly_role (615-623) issues `ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES FOR ROLE CURRENT_USER IN SCHEMA {schema} GRANT SELECT ON TABLES TO {schema}_ro`. dbt creates the materialized table as that same CURRENT_USER (the managed role from MANAGED_DATABASE_URL) inside the attacker's own schema, so the attacker's _ro role automatically gains SELECT on it. A model body of `SELECT * FROM t_victim.raw_cases` therefore copies another tenant's data into the attacker's schema and is then readable through the ordinary read-only retrieval path — a complete bypass of the SET ROLE _ro + validator isolation the query path enforces. The managed role's CREATEROLE capability (proven by line 605) also enables role/privilege manipulation via jinja run_query.

Every file:line in the finding checks out (serializers.py:6, views.py:74/121, models.py:100-114, executor.py:134, dbt_runner.py:28, query.py:44).
votes.notes:
Minor wording nit in the finding, not affecting the verdict: it says "models.py:100 validates only name." Line 100-102 is save()/full_clean(); `name` is validated by the field-level RegexValidator (lines 26-29) and clean() (104-114) validates scope/container consistency. The substantive point — that sql_content has zero validation — is correct.

Severity stands at security / BROKEN-NOW. The only practical hurdle for the cross-tenant SELECT variant is knowing the victim's schema name (derived from tenant_id via _sanitize_schema_name: lowercased, dashes->underscores), but that is not a real barrier: the same arbitrary-SQL primitive can enumerate pg_namespace via jinja run_query, and the DDL/CREATEROLE capability is independent of knowing any victim name. Reachability is solid via two live API routes plus automatic execution during every materialization's transform stage.

title: dbt staging models can't resolve raw tables: generated SQL is unqualified and the dbt profile sets no search_path — every generated CommCare staging model fails silently
claim: The CommCare staging generator emits model SQL with unqualified FROM raw_cases/FROM raw_forms, but generate_profiles_yml sets no search_path and dbt-postgres only applies one from the profile, so dbt creates the model relation in the tenant schema while executing the SELECT body against the default search_path ('$user', public) where the raw tables do not exist — every generated staging model fails at dbt runtime with 'relation does not exist'. The breakage is triply silent: transform failures are isolated (no re-raise), the materializer still marks the run COMPLETED, the resume aggregator reads only result['sources'] (never result['transforms']), the agent is told 'completed', the ThreadJob goes green, and all dbt tests are mocked. Same swallowed-failure class as the view-schema variant fixed in PR #229, but the transform variant was not. System-asset generation failures are also swallowed ('continuing pipeline') and compose with this.
chain: materializer.py:217 commcare assets generated -> :452 _run_transform_phase -> executor.py:134 db_url=MANAGED_DATABASE_URL -> dbt_runner.py:28-61 no search_path -> commcare_staging.py:144 'FROM raw_cases' unqualified -> dbt CREATE TABLE t_<id>.stg_x AS (SELECT ... FROM raw_cases) resolved against $user,public -> relation does not exist; swallowed: executor.py:85-92 + materializer.py:455-477 COMPLETED + tasks.py:961-982 ignores transforms

votes.evidence:
Independently re-traced chain:

1. SQL is unqualified: apps/transformations/services/commcare_staging.py:144 `lines.append("FROM raw_cases")` and :198 `lines.append("FROM raw_forms")`. Repeat-group models (:242) use `{{ ref('<parent>') }}` so they depend on the failing parents.

2. Raw tables exist ONLY in the tenant schema: mcp_server/services/materializer.py:1122-1126 `CREATE TABLE {schema}.raw_cases (...)` and :1189-1193 `CREATE TABLE {schema}.raw_forms (...)` — nothing creates them in public.

3. The dbt project writes asset SQL verbatim: apps/transformations/services/dbt_project.py:49-50 `(models_dir / f"{asset.name}.sql").write_text(asset.sql_content)` — no source() qualification, no schema injection, no hooks.

4. The profile sets no search_path: mcp_server/services/dbt_runner.py:43-58 — profile dict contains only type/host/port/user/password/dbname/schema/threads. The `schema` key controls where dbt CREATEs the relation, not how the SELECT body resolves names.

5. Installed dbt-postgres 1.10.0 (.venv/lib/python3.13/site-packages/dbt/adapters/postgres/connections.py:114-119) applies search_path ONLY when set in credentials: `if search_path is not None and search_path != "": kwargs["options"] = "-c search_path={}"`. Absent that, the connection uses the role/db default ('$user', public). Tenant schemas are dynamically named, so no static role config could include them. (Corroborating: the MCP query path explicitly sets search_path per-connection — mcp_server/context.py:159, mcp_server/services/query.py:47,77 — the dbt path is the one place that doesn't.)

6. Therefore `create table "<db>"."<tenant_schema>"."stg_case_x" as (SELECT ... FROM raw_cases ...)` fails with 'relation "raw_cases" does not exist' for every case/form staging model; repeat-group models skip/fail on the broken ref.

7. Swallowing, layer by layer (all verified):
   - dbt_runner.py:100-103 returns `{"success": False, "error": ..., "models": {}}` — no exception.
   - executor.py:_execute_stage 165-176 marks each TransformationAssetRun FAILED, :184-185 only `logger.warning` — does NOT raise. So executor.py:81-83 marks the TransformationRun COMPLETED (the :85-92 except handler is never reached for this failure mode).
   - materializer.py:1062-1074 `_run_transform_phase` returns `{"status": "completed", ...}` with NO "error" key (run.error_message is empty), so materializer.py:497/506-507 never attaches `transform_error` either.
   - materializer.py:471-477 marks MaterializationRun COMPLETED with `result={"sources":..., "transforms":...}`.
   - apps/workspaces/tasks.py:961-1016 `_aggregate_materialization_state` iterates only `r.result.get("sources")` (:966) — transforms never read; run state COMPLETED → status "completed" (:1006-1007); the resume agent and ThreadJob see success.

8. Tests mocked: tests/test_dbt_runner.py mocks dbtRunner via MagicMock; tests/test_transformation_executor.py patches `apps.transformations.services.executor.run_dbt` and `generate_profiles_yml` in every test (lines 82-83, 95-96, 115-116, 135...). No test executes real dbt against a real schema.

9. Reachability: live path. pipelines/commcare_sync.yml declares provider "commcare"; apps/workspaces/tasks.py:166-172/248-275 maps provider→pipeline via the registry inside the `materialize_workspace` Procrastinate task; materializer.py:217-229 generates system assets for provider=="commcare" during DISCOVER; :448-454 runs the transform phase whenever the tenant has any TransformationAsset. CommCare OAuth tenants exist (apps/users/services/tenant_resolution.py:47-53). System-asset generation swallowing also confirmed at materializer.py:230-234 ("continuing pipeline").
votes.notes: Finding is correct end-to-end; one mechanism refinement makes it WORSE than stated. The finding attributes the swallow to executor.py:85-92 (catch + mark FAILED + no re-raise). In the actual failure mode that handler never fires: run_dbt returns success=False without raising, so _execute_stage just logs a warning and the TransformationRun itself is marked COMPLETED (executor.py:81-83) — not FAILED. Consequence: even the materializer's existing transform_error surfacing (materializer.py:497,506-507, which would put transform_error into the agent-visible result) is bypassed, because _run_transform_phase only sets "error" from run.error_message, which is only populated on the exception path. The sole durable failure record is per-asset TransformationAssetRun rows (status FAILED, logs) plus a logger.warning — nothing user- or agent-visible. The executor:85-92 swallow path is real but applies to a different failure class (e.g., write_dbt_project/profile errors). Minor scope note: repeat-group models fail/skip transitively via ref() rather than directly via unqualified raw tables, so "every generated staging model fails" holds in effect. I did not run dbt against a live Postgres to reproduce the error string, but the adapter source (dbt-postgres 1.10.0 connections.py) plus Postgres default search_path semantics leave no alternative resolution path; the only escape hatch would be an out-of-band ALTER ROLE/DATABASE SET search_path naming each dynamic tenant schema, which cannot exist for dynamically provisioned schemas and appears nowhere in the codebase.

title: Workspace-scope transformations never run during materialization; stale dbt tables presented as fresh; orphaned system assets keep executing
claim: _run_transform_phase calls run_transformation_pipeline without a workspace argument and the executor only appends the workspace stage when workspace is truthy, so scope=WORKSPACE TransformationAssets are executed only by the manual /runs/trigger/ endpoint (no frontend UI) — creatable via API but never materialized in the shipped product. Because dbt assets are materialized as tables (+materialized:table), a workspace-scoped model's table survives raw-table reloads with pre-refresh contents and (multi-tenant) gets re-published via the view schema next to fresh data with no staleness marker. Separately, upsert_system_assets only update_or_creates and never deletes assets the latest metadata no longer produces, so a renamed/removed CommCare form/case-type leaves an orphan stg_* asset that keeps being selected, handed to dbt, and listed to the agent (stale table or silent failure).

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced all three chains by reading the actual files.

CHAIN 1 — workspace-scoped transforms never run during materialization:
- executor.py:62-69: the WORKSPACE stage is only appended when `if workspace:` is truthy. The base `stages` list (lines 50-61) contains only SYSTEM and TENANT.
- executor.py:73: each stage selects `list(TransformationAsset.objects.filter(**filters))` where the workspace stage filter is `{"workspace": workspace, "scope": WORKSPACE}` — so scope=WORKSPACE assets are only fetched/run if a workspace is passed.
- materializer.py:1060-1065: `_run_transform_phase(pipeline, schema_name, tenant=None)` calls `run_transformation_pipeline(tenant=tenant, schema_name=schema_name)` — NO workspace argument, so workspace defaults to None in executor.py:35.
- materializer.py:452-454: the live materialize path calls `_run_transform_phase(pipeline, schema_name, tenant=tenant_membership.tenant)` — no workspace threaded in. I grepped materializer.py for "workspace" as a param/var: it appears ONLY in an import (line 55) and never as a function parameter — materialization is purely tenant-scoped, with no plumbing to even obtain a workspace.
- views.py:148-163: the ONLY call site that passes `workspace=workspace` is the manual `@action POST /api/transformations/runs/trigger/` endpoint.
- Reachability: I confirmed the frontend has NO transformations API usage. `frontend/src/api/` contains only auth.ts/client.ts/jobs.ts/threads.ts/userTenantsCache.ts/workspaces.ts — no transformations module. grep for `/api/transformations` / `runs/trigger` / `TransformationAsset` across frontend/src returned zero API references (only unrelated radix-ui `data-slot=*-trigger` UI primitives and recipes runs). There is no `apps/transformations/tasks.py` and no scheduled/procrastinate task calls `run_transformation_pipeline`. So scope=WORKSPACE assets are creatable via the CRUD API (views.py:54-59 allow non-SYSTEM creation) but never materialized in the shipped product — exactly as claimed.

CHAIN 2 — stale dbt tables presented as fresh + multi-tenant re-publish:
- dbt_project.py:44: `"models": {"+materialized": "table"}` (corroborated by tests/test_dbt_project_writer.py:27). So dbt models are tables, which survive raw-table ON CONFLICT reloads (materializer.py raw_cases/raw_forms upserts at 1081-1109).
- schema_manager.py build_view_schema (Step 2, lines 327-344) selects table_name from `information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema = %s AND table_type IN ('BASE TABLE','VIEW')` — an UNFILTERED enumeration of the tenant schema. Every table, including a leftover workspace-scoped dbt table, becomes `CREATE VIEW {view_schema}.{prefix__table} AS SELECT * FROM {tenant_schema}.{table}` (lines 367-375). No staleness marker / no allowlist. This confirms the claim that a stale workspace-model table would be re-published next to fresh raw data.

CHAIN 3 — orphaned system assets keep executing:
- commcare_staging.py:333-347: `upsert_system_assets` only does `TransformationAsset.objects.update_or_create(name=..., scope=SYSTEM, tenant=...)` per generated asset. No delete/exclude/prune. I grepped commcare_staging.py — only `generate_system_assets` and `upsert_system_assets` exist; no prune/reconcile function. The only asset deletion in the whole transformations app is the manual CRUD `instance.delete()` in views.py:72 (perform_destroy). So a renamed/removed CommCare form leaves the old `stg_*` asset in the DB, which is then re-selected at executor.py:73 (`filter(scope=SYSTEM, tenant=...)`), handed to dbt (executor.py:144-149), and surfaced to the agent via metadata listing (metadata.py references TransformationAsset). Confirmed.
votes.notes: All three mechanisms independently reproduced; the file:line references in the finding are accurate. Status LATENT and impact correctness are correct: the workspace-transform gap and orphan-asset gap are real defects in shipped code but the workspace path is only reachable via an API endpoint with no UI/scheduler caller (so no user is hitting it today), and the orphan path requires the specific event of a form/case-type being renamed or removed upstream. Both are dormant-but-real, matching LATENT. One nuance worth noting (does not change the verdict): the agent-facing 'stale table presented as fresh' consequence for Chain 1+2 only fully manifests if a workspace-scoped asset was at some point successfully materialized (e.g. via the manual trigger endpoint) and later orphaned by raw reloads; in a deployment where the trigger endpoint is never called, the workspace-model table never gets created in the first place, so the practical surface today is dominated by Chain 3 (orphan system assets) plus the design-level fact that workspace transforms silently do nothing. This is a severity nuance, not a refutation — the code does exactly what the finding says.

title: dbt model names and column aliases from CommCare metadata have no 63-byte guard; synchronous /runs/trigger/ blocks the request thread with a per-process lock
claim: commcare_staging builds dbt model names (stg_case_{slug}, {parent}__repeat_{group_slug}) and column aliases from provider-controlled metadata; slugify bounds shape not length, TransformationAsset.name is CharField(255) with a shape-only regex, and dbt materializes each as a relation truncated by Postgres to 63 bytes — two long form/case names sharing the first ~63 bytes produce two asset rows but one physical relation (dbt builds one over the other, ref() ambiguous), and _unique_alias disambiguates in Python before truncation so long property names still collide. Separately, TransformationRunViewSet.trigger is a synchronous DRF action running dbt inline under a process-global threading.Lock, blocking the serving thread for the full dbt duration; the lock is per-process so a worker-side materialization transform and an API-side trigger on the same tenant schema are not serialized, racing DROP/CREATE TABLE DDL. The entire dbt-execution path has zero non-mocked test coverage.

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced all sub-claims against the actual code; line numbers in the finding are accurate.

1) Provider-controlled model names with NO length guard:
- commcare_staging.py:147 `model_name = f"stg_case_{slugify_model_name(case_type_name)}"`; :201 `f"stg_form_{model_name_slug}"`; :248 `f"{parent_model}__repeat_{group_slug}"`. The slug comes from slugify_model_name (:48-65), which lowercases/strips/collapses but does NOT bound length.
- models.py:23-32: `name = CharField(max_length=255, validators=[RegexValidator(r"^[a-z][a-z0-9_]*$")])` — shape-only, length up to 255. Unique constraints (models.py:83-92) are on the full `name` (`fields=["name","scope","tenant"]`).
- dbt_project.py:49 writes one file per asset `models/{asset.name}.sql` and project_config materialized=table (dbt_project.py:44) → each asset becomes one Postgres relation. Postgres NAMEDATALEN truncates identifiers to 63 bytes. `grep -rn "63|truncat|NAMEDATALEN"` across apps/transformations and materializer/dbt_runner returns ZERO guards. So two case/form names sharing the first ~63 bytes produce two distinct asset rows (distinct 255-char names, distinct .sql files) but ONE physical 63-byte relation — dbt builds one over the other and ref() is ambiguous. Confirmed.

2) _unique_alias disambiguates pre-truncation:
- commcare_staging.py:89-95 appends `_2`,`_3` on Python-string collisions only. Column aliases are emitted with the full slug (:141, :195, :239). Postgres also truncates column identifiers to 63 bytes, so two property names diverging only after byte 63 get distinct Python aliases but collapse to the same physical column. _unique_alias cannot catch this. Confirmed.

3) Synchronous trigger blocking the serving thread under a process-global lock:
- views.py:121-122 `@action(detail=False, methods=["post"]) def trigger(self, request):` — a plain SYNC def on a DRF ReadOnlyModelViewSet. Routed live: apps/transformations/urls.py:8 registers the viewset; config/urls.py:96 includes it at /api/transformations/. It calls run_transformation_pipeline inline (views.py:159) → executor.run_transformation_pipeline (executor.py:32) → _execute_stage → run_dbt (executor.py:145).
- dbt_runner.py:25 `_dbt_lock = threading.Lock()` (module-level), acquired at :96 and :143. A threading.Lock is process-local.

4) Per-process lock does NOT serialize worker vs API:
- Worker path: apps/workspaces/tasks.py:173 `await asyncio.to_thread(run_pipeline, ...)` inside the procrastinate `materialize_workspace` task (runs in `manage.py procrastinate worker`, a SEPARATE process from uvicorn). run_pipeline → materializer.py:452 _run_transform_phase → materializer.py:1062 run_transformation_pipeline(schema_name) → run_dbt.
- API path runs the same run_transformation_pipeline in the API process on the same tenant schema (views.py:140 `TenantSchema.objects.filter(tenant=tenant, state="active")`, :161 `schema_name=ts.schema_name`). Both issue DROP/CREATE TABLE DDL (materialized=table) against the same Postgres schema. Two processes, two distinct _dbt_lock instances → no cross-process serialization. Race confirmed.

5) Zero non-mocked dbt-execution coverage:
- tests/test_dbt_runner.py: every TestRunDbt case patches `mcp_server.services.dbt_runner.dbtRunner` (lines 63,85,105,138).
- tests/test_transformation_executor.py: every test patches `executor.run_dbt`/`run_dbt_test`/`generate_profiles_yml` (lines 82-83,95-96,…).
- tests/test_transformation_api.py:391 test_trigger_run patches `apps.transformations.services.executor.run_transformation_pipeline` entirely. The generator tests (tests/test_commcare_staging_generator.py) only assert SQL string contents; none run dbt. So dbt is never invoked against a real Postgres. Confirmed.
votes.notes:
Verdict CONFIRMED, status LATENT and impact correctness both stand. Two reachability nuances worth recording (they refine, not refute):

- The /runs/trigger/ endpoint is HTTP-reachable by any authenticated tenant member (route is live), but I grep'd frontend/src and found NO reference to the transformations API at all — it is not wired to any UI element today. The dominant live dbt path is the worker-side materialization, reachable via the `run_materialization` MCP tool → `materialize_workspace` procrastinate task. So the synchronous-blocking + cross-process-race scenario requires someone to POST /runs/trigger/ directly (e.g., script/curl) concurrently with a worker materialization on the same tenant — plausible but not a default UI flow. This supports the LATENT (not BROKEN-NOW) classification.

- The truncation-collision defects (model names and column aliases) are genuinely latent: they need provider metadata where two case/form/property names share the first 63 bytes. Realistic for machine-generated CommCare app builders but not the common case. LATENT is correct.

The finding's mechanism, files, and line numbers are all accurate. No corrections to status or impact needed.

title: Onboarding 'Use an API Key' form POSTs to the deleted /api/auth/tenant-credentials/ endpoint — guaranteed 404 on the critical first-run path
claim: Every new user without OAuth is forced into OnboardingWizard (onboarding_complete is false until a connection-backed TenantMembership exists), whose 'Use an API Key' form POSTs to /api/auth/tenant-credentials/ with the pre-#220 payload shape. That route was removed in the TenantConnection rebuild (ad56a65, replaced by /api/auth/connections/ with {provider, fields}), so the wizard guarantees a 404 -> 'Failed to save credentials' dead end. The wizard is a pre-rebuild fossil that also only offers CommCare while ApiConnectionDialog supports the full provider registry; the bug survives because the frontend API types are hand-written.
chain: App.tsx:65 renders OnboardingWizard when !onboarding_complete (auth_views.py:72-90) -> OnboardingWizard.tsx:27 api.post('/api/auth/tenant-credentials/', {provider,tenant_id,tenant_name,credential}) -> apps/users/auth_urls.py (no such path; only connections/) -> 404 -> OnboardingWizard.tsx:36-37 'Failed to save credentials'

votes.evidence:
Re-traced the full chain from entry point to consequence:

1. REACHABILITY (live, root-gated): frontend/src/App.tsx:65-67 — `if (authStatus === "authenticated" && user && !user.onboarding_complete) { return <OnboardingWizard /> }`. This is the root component gate, not a buried route; every authenticated user without onboarding hits it.

2. WHO HAS onboarding_complete=false: apps/users/auth_views.py:72-76 — `onboarding_complete = await TenantMembership.objects.filter(user=user, connection__isnull=False, archived_at__isnull=True).aexists()`. Lines 82-90 attempt lazy OAuth resolution, but auth_views.py:47-50 (`_atry_resolve_provider`) returns False immediately when `aget_social_token` finds no token — so a new email/password user with no OAuth has no escape; wizard renders.

3. THE BROKEN POST: frontend/src/components/OnboardingWizard/OnboardingWizard.tsx:27-32 — `await api.post("/api/auth/tenant-credentials/", { provider: "commcare", tenant_id: domain, tenant_name: domain, credential: \`${username}:${apiKey}\` })`.

4. ROUTE DOES NOT EXIST: config/urls.py:99 mounts `path("api/auth/", include("apps.users.auth_urls"))`; apps/users/auth_urls.py:25-47 contains only csrf/, me/, login/, logout/, providers/, signup/, tenants/*, connections/, connections/<id>/, api-key-providers/ — no `tenant-credentials/`. No catch-all exists under /api/ in config/urls.py:78-111. Guaranteed Django 404.

5. REMOVAL COMMIT VERIFIED: `git show ad56a65` diff of apps/users/auth_urls.py shows `-path("tenant-credentials/", ...)` → `+path("connections/", ...)`, commit message: "/api/auth/connections/ list/create/rotate/remove (replaces tenant-credentials)".

6. PAYLOAD ALSO STALE: apps/users/views.py:222-241 — the replacement POST handler reads `body.get("provider")` and `body.get("fields")` and validates against STRATEGIES form_fields; the wizard's `{tenant_id, tenant_name, credential}` shape would fail with 400 even if the URL were patched.

7. USER-VISIBLE FAILURE: frontend/src/api/client.ts:37-40 — `if (!res.ok) throw new ApiError(res.status, body.detail ?? body.error ?? res.statusText)`; OnboardingWizard.tsx:36-37 catches and renders the error. Form is a dead end for the API-key path.
votes.notes: Two minor nuances, neither severity-changing: (1) The displayed error will be the ApiError message (likely "Not Found") rather than the literal fallback "Failed to save credentials" — ApiError extends Error so `err.message` wins at OnboardingWizard.tsx:37. (2) The wizard is not a total dead end for all users: the "Connect with OAuth" button (OnboardingWizard.tsx:127, allauth route at /accounts/commcare/login/) still works — but the finding already scopes the claim to non-OAuth users, and for them the API-key form is the only in-wizard option and it is guaranteed broken. Also confirmed the secondary observation: the wizard hardcodes CommCare only, while the live registry (apps/users/services/api_key_providers/registry.py) supports commcare + ocs with a {provider, fields} contract. No test coverage caught this: tests/qa/ has only a data-dictionary scenario; backend tests were migrated to the connection model in ad56a65 but the wizard frontend was not.

votes.evidence:
Independently re-traced the full chain:

1. GATE: frontend/src/App.tsx:65-66 — `if (authStatus === "authenticated" && user && !user.onboarding_complete) { return <OnboardingWizard /> }`. The router (and thus ConnectionsPage/ApiConnectionDialog, which do use the correct new endpoint at frontend/src/pages/ConnectionsPage/ConnectionsPage.tsx:92 and ApiConnectionDialog.tsx:97) never mounts until onboarding completes, so there is no workaround in the UI.

2. WHO HITS IT: apps/users/auth_views.py:72-76 — `onboarding_complete = await TenantMembership.objects.filter(user=user, connection__isnull=False, archived_at__isnull=True).aexists()`; lines 82-90 only flip it via lazy OAuth resolution, which requires an existing social token (`aget_social_token` in `_atry_resolve_provider`, lines 46-56). An email/password signup user (signup_view is live at auth_urls.py:36) with no OAuth token gets onboarding_complete=false and lands in the wizard.

3. THE BROKEN CALL: frontend/src/components/OnboardingWizard/OnboardingWizard.tsx:27-32 — `await api.post("/api/auth/tenant-credentials/", { provider: "commcare", tenant_id: domain, tenant_name: domain, credential: `${username}:${apiKey}` })` — the pre-rebuild payload shape.

4. NO SUCH ROUTE: apps/users/auth_urls.py:25-47 contains only csrf/me/login/logout/providers/signup/tenants/connections/api-key-providers. config/urls.py:99 mounts it at `api/auth/`, and config/urls.py has no SPA catch-all after it (file ends at line 111), so the request is a hard Django 404. `grep -rn "tenant-credentials" apps/ config/` returns zero backend hits.

5. HISTORY: commit ad56a65 (2026-06-05, "feat(users): connection endpoints...") commit message explicitly says "/api/auth/connections/ list/create/rotate/remove (replaces tenant-credentials)". New POST shape is `{provider, fields}` (apps/users/views.py:226-227), incompatible with the wizard's payload even if the URL were patched.

6. FAILURE SURFACE: frontend/src/api/client.ts:37-39 throws `ApiError(404, ...)`; OnboardingWizard.tsx:36-37 catches it. Minor correction: since ApiError extends Error, the user sees `err.message` ("Not Found"), not the literal "Failed to save credentials" fallback — but it is the same dead end either way.

Reachability confirmed live: the wizard is wired to the authenticated app shell today; only its "Connect with OAuth" branch (OnboardingWizard.tsx:127, allauth redirect) works. The "Use an API Key" branch (the only non-OAuth path through first-run) is guaranteed broken. Also confirmed the finding's secondary point: the wizard hardcodes provider "commcare" while the new endpoint supports the full STRATEGIES registry (views.py:229).
votes.notes: Two refinements, neither changing the verdict: (1) the user-visible error is "Not Found" (ApiError.message propagates), not the "Failed to save credentials" fallback string the finding quotes — that fallback only fires for non-Error throws; (2) scope nuance: users who pick the wizard's OAuth button are unaffected, so the dead end specifically hits API-key-first users and any deployment where CommCare OAuth isn't configured — for those, it is a total first-run lockout since the router never mounts pre-onboarding. Fix likely needs both the URL and the payload shape ({provider, fields:{...}} per views.py:226-245), or replacing the wizard's form with the existing ApiConnectionDialog. Note the same fossil exists in .claude/worktrees/integration-incident-fixes.

title: BASE_PATH-bypassing root-relative URLs break labs /scout deployment: health poll, artifact sandbox iframe, and public share pages
claim: Several frontend/backend URLs are hardcoded root-relative without the BASE_PATH prefix, breaking the labs deployment built with VITE_BASE_PATH=/scout/ (nginx only proxies /scout/...). (1) NetworkStatusContext fetches '/health/' raw, so the 5s health poll monitors whatever the labs host serves at root /health/ — the network indicator is permanently green-while-down or red-with-suppression of all page error states (dishonest-indicator class). (2) ArtifactPanel's sandbox iframe src and the Django-served sandbox HTML's /api/ fetch are root-relative, so the artifact View tab 404s in the main app and embed widget under /scout. (3) PublicThreadPage/PublicRecipeRunPage re-derive the share token from the unstripped pathname with a regex anchored at ^/shared/, so under /scout the token is undefined, the fetch returns early, and every public share link renders an endless skeleton. Root-mounted Kamal prod is unaffected. nginx.prod-kamal.conf also lacks a /widget.js location so the embed SDK URL 404s on scout.dimagi.com.

votes.evidence:
Independently re-traced all four sub-claims.

(1) Health poll — CONFIRMED. frontend/src/contexts/NetworkStatusContext.tsx:29: `const res = await fetch("/health/", { method: "GET", cache: "no-store" })` — raw root-relative, while every other API call goes through `${BASE_PATH}` prefixing (frontend/src/api/client.ts:30 `url.startsWith("/") ? \`${BASE_PATH}${url}\` : url`). Labs build sets `VITE_BASE_PATH=/scout/` (.github/workflows/deploy-labs.yml:200) and uses frontend/nginx.prod.conf (deploy-labs.yml:201), which defines ONLY `/scout/...` locations — health is at `location /scout/health/` (proxy to localhost:8000/health/); there is no `location /health/` or `location /`, so a root `/health/` request hits the server-level `root /usr/share/nginx/html` and 404s (or never reaches this nginx if the labs ALB routes only /scout*). Result: `res.ok` false → permanent `status === "offline"`. Consequence chain confirmed: AppLayout.tsx:64 renders OfflineBanner ("Server unreachable — attempting to reconnect...", shown whenever status !== "online"), and page error states are gated on a green indicator, e.g. KnowledgePage.tsx:182 `{knowledgeStatus === "error" && networkStatus === "online" && (...Failed to load...)}` — so real page errors are suppressed while the false offline banner shows. The "green-while-down" branch is also possible if whatever the labs host serves at root /health/ returns 200. Reachable: NetworkStatusProvider wraps the authenticated RouterProvider in App.tsx and polls every 5s.

(2) Artifact sandbox — CONFIRMED, both layers. frontend/src/components/ArtifactPanel/ArtifactPanel.tsx:192: `src={activeDomainId ? \`/api/workspaces/${activeDomainId}/artifacts/${artifactId}/sandbox/\` : ""}` — root-relative iframe src, 404s under /scout (nginx only proxies /scout/api/). ArtifactPanel is live in both AppLayout.tsx:63 (main app) and EmbedLayout.tsx:26 (embed widget). Second layer: apps/artifacts/views.py:254, the Django-served sandbox HTML's inline JS does `fetch('/api/workspaces/' + artifact.workspace_id + ... + '/query-data/')` — root-relative; Django sits behind the /scout-stripping proxy and has no knowledge of the prefix, so even with a fixed iframe src, live-query artifacts would fail at root /api/. (This second layer only triggers for `artifact.has_live_queries`.)

(3) Public share pages — CONFIRMED. App.tsx:15-16 stripBasePath correctly strips "/scout" for route matching, so PublicThreadPage/PublicRecipeRunPage DO render under /scout/shared/.... But each page re-derives the token from the UNstripped pathname: PublicThreadPage.tsx:57-60 `window.location.pathname.match(/^\/shared\/threads\/([^/]+)/)` and PublicRecipeRunPage.tsx:49-53 `match(/^\/shared\/runs\/([^/]+)/)`. Under /scout the pathname is `/scout/shared/...`, the anchored regex fails, token is undefined, the `useEffect` returns early (`if (!token) return` — PublicThreadPage.tsx:201, PublicRecipeRunPage.tsx:62), `loading` stays true forever → permanent skeleton. (The fetch itself at PublicThreadPage.tsx:202 / PublicRecipeRunPage.tsx:63 is correctly BASE_PATH-prefixed; only token derivation is broken.)

(4) widget.js on kamal prod — REFUTED. frontend/public/widget.js exists as a static asset, so Vite copies it to the dist root and nginx.prod-kamal.conf's `location / { try_files $uri $uri/ /index.html; }` serves /widget.js directly (root /usr/share/nginx/html). No dedicated location is required for it to resolve; the dedicated block in nginx.prod.conf exists for the /scout alias plus CORS/cache headers. What kamal loses vs prod.conf is only `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` and Cache-Control — and widget.js self-detects its base from script src (widget.js:6-18) and is loaded as a classic <script>, which needs no CORS. So no 404 on scout.dimagi.com.

Root-mounted kamal prod unaffected by (1)-(3): BASE_PATH defaults to "" (frontend/src/config.ts:9), making all the broken URLs correct at root.
votes.notes: Verdict is PARTIAL only because the nginx.prod-kamal.conf /widget.js sub-claim is wrong (file is served by the catch-all try_files; worst case is missing CORS/cache headers, which classic script-tag loading does not need). Sub-claims 1-3 are fully confirmed with reachable live paths on the labs /scout deployment: 5s health poll from every authenticated page, artifact View tab in main app + embed widget, and both public share page types. Severity/status/impact as filed (BROKEN-NOW / correctness) are accurate for the labs deployment. One refinement: in failure mode (1), which branch occurs (permanently-red vs permanently-green) depends on what the labs host serves at root /health/; either way the indicator is dishonest and the red branch additionally suppresses real page error banners (KnowledgePage, RecipesPage, DataDictionaryPage, ArtifactsPage all gate errors on networkStatus === "online"). Fix surface is small: route the health fetch through the BASE_PATH-aware client, prefix the iframe src with BASE_PATH, derive share tokens from the stripped path (or pass via props from App.tsx), and inject a base path into the sandbox HTML template (Django can't infer it behind the stripping proxy).

title: Artifacts and Recipes pages don't refetch on workspace switch: stale cross-workspace data then 404 actions
claim: RecipesPage and ArtifactsPage fetch only on mount (effect deps exclude activeDomainId; zustand actions are stable refs), while KnowledgePage/DataDictionaryPage correctly refetch on workspace change. Switching workspaces via the always-present TopBar switcher while on /recipes or /artifacts leaves the previous workspace's items on screen; subsequent actions (fetchRecipe, runRecipe, openArtifact->sandbox) combine the NEW activeDomainId with OLD object ids and 404. The threadId-leak incident fix (00c423d) reset only threadId in setActiveDomain; no general per-workspace state reset exists. Same problem solved two different ways across four sibling pages.
chain: TopBar workspace switcher -> WorkspaceSwitcher select() setActiveDomain -> RecipesPage.tsx:45-47 useEffect deps [fetchRecipes] only -> list still workspace A's -> recipeSlice fetchRecipe reads new activeDomainId -> GET /api/workspaces/<B>/recipes/<recipe-of-A>/ -> apps/recipes/api/views.py:55-57 404

votes.evidence:
Re-traced independently, all links hold:

1. REACHABILITY — switcher is live on these routes. frontend/src/router.tsx:30,34-36 wires `/artifacts` and `/recipes[/:id[/runs/:runId]]` under AppLayout; AppLayout.tsx:55-61 wraps the Outlet in TopBarProvider, and TopBar.tsx:23 renders `<WorkspaceSwitcher variant="topbar" />` unconditionally. WorkspaceSwitcher.tsx:260-274 `select(ws)` calls `setActiveDomain(ws.id)` and `newThread()`, and only navigates away when `location.pathname.startsWith(wsDetailPrefix)` (i.e. /workspaces/... detail pages) — on /recipes or /artifacts the user stays put. `newThread()` (uiSlice.ts:50-52) only sets state, no navigation. `useWorkspaceThreadSync` (the only hook that navigates on activeDomainId change) is mounted solely in ChatRoute.tsx, so it does not fire on these pages.

2. NO RESET, NO REMOUNT — domainSlice.ts:53-70 `setActiveDomain` sets only `{activeDomainId, threadId: crypto.randomUUID()}`; no recipes/artifacts state is cleared. Commit 00c423d exists ("fix(chat): stop carrying threads across workspaces and recover from stale threads") and matches the claim that only threadId was reset. AppLayout's Outlet is not keyed by activeDomainId and WorkspaceJobsContext.tsx:20-21 is a plain context provider (no key), so RecipesPage/ArtifactsPage do not remount on switch.

3. STALE FETCH — RecipesPage.tsx:45-47: `useEffect(() => { fetchRecipes() }, [fetchRecipes])`. ArtifactsPage.tsx:21-25: deps `[artifactSearch, fetchArtifacts]`. Both omit activeDomainId. The action objects (`recipeActions`, `artifactActions`) are created once in the slice creators and never replaced by any `set()` call, so the function refs are stable and the effects never re-run on workspace switch. Workspace A's list stays rendered.

4. INCONSISTENT SIBLINGS — KnowledgePage.tsx:56 deps `[activeDomainId, knowledgeFilter, knowledgeSearch, fetchKnowledge]` and DataDictionaryPage.tsx:27 deps `[fetchDictionary, clearDictionary, activeDomainId]` both correctly refetch on switch. Same problem, two patterns across four sibling pages — confirmed.

5. 404 CONSEQUENCE — recipeSlice.ts:96-107 `fetchRecipe` and :132-141 `runRecipe` read `get().activeDomainId` fresh (now workspace B) and hit `/api/workspaces/${activeDomainId}/recipes/${recipeId}/...` with workspace A's recipe id. Backend apps/recipes/api/views.py `RecipeDetailView._get_recipe` does `Recipe.objects.get(pk=recipe_id, workspace=workspace)` and returns `{"error": "Recipe not found."}` 404 on DoesNotExist — cross-workspace id scoping guarantees the 404. Artifact path identical: ArtifactPanel.tsx:53-64 query-data fetch and :192 sandbox iframe `src` both combine `activeDomainId` (B) with the stale artifact id (A).
votes.notes: Two minor nuances, neither changing the verdict: (a) ArtifactsPage partially self-heals — typing in the search box changes `artifactSearch`, re-running the effect and fetching workspace B's artifacts; RecipesPage has no such escape hatch short of a full reload or visiting another page and returning (remount). (b) AppLayout.tsx:28-48 has an online-recovery effect that refetches errored slices, but it only fires on offline→online transitions, so it does not mitigate the switch case. Backend scoping is correct (the 404 is the server doing its job); the defect is purely frontend staleness plus the absence of a general per-workspace state reset in setActiveDomain.
